items.title: DHS proposes raising naturalization application fees
items.summary: DHS published a proposed rule to raise the Form N-400 citizenship application fee and end most fee waivers.
items.details: On June 22-23, 2026, the Department of Homeland Security proposed raising the paper Form N-400 fee from $760 to $1,330 and the online fee from $710 to $1,280, and eliminating reduced-fee and waiver options for most applicants. Military service members remain exempt. A 60-day public comment period runs through August 24, 2026.

items.title: Executive order on advanced AI innovation and security
items.summary: The White House issued an executive order directing federal agencies to strengthen cyber defenses related to advanced AI.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, the White House issued Executive Order 14409, directing agencies to act within 30 days to prioritize cyber defense, establishing a voluntary framework for frontier AI model deployment, and directing enforcement resources toward criminal misuse of AI.

items.title: GAO reports on federal workforce changes
items.summary: A GAO report documented declines in the federal workforce across major agencies during 2025.
items.details: In a June 17, 2026 report, GAO stated that nearly 378,000 employees separated from 22 major agencies during 2025 while about 127,000 were hired. The total workforce across these agencies declined by nearly 256,000 employees, or more than 11 percent, from December 2024 to January 2026.

items.title: U.S. and Iran hold talks under signed memorandum of understanding
items.summary: U.S. and Iranian delegations met in Switzerland to advance a framework intended to end the 2026 Iran war.
items.details: On June 17, 2026, the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding establishing a 60-day ceasefire extension to negotiate final terms. Subsequent talks addressed the Strait of Hormuz, sanctions relief, and Iran's nuclear program, while fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon complicated the process.

items.title: Israel and Hezbollah renew Lebanon ceasefire
items.summary: Israel and Hezbollah agreed to renew a ceasefire after intensified fighting in southern Lebanon.
items.details: Around June 19, 2026, Israel and Hezbollah agreed to renew their ceasefire following a deadly escalation in southern Lebanon. The truce was mediated by Qatar, the U.S., and Iran. Both sides subsequently accused each other of violations, and U.S.-Iran talks were briefly disrupted.

items.title: State Department announces Ethiopia visa restrictions
items.summary: The State Department announced targeted visa restrictions for individuals it said were involved in undermining peace in Ethiopia.
items.details: On June 18, 2026, the State Department announced targeted visa restrictions for individuals involved in undermining peace in Ethiopia, alongside other press statements including reward offers related to the foreign terrorist organization MS-13.

items.title: Congress passes housing affordability bill
items.summary: Congress passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, described as the largest housing package in decades.
items.details: The Senate passed the bill on June 22, 2026, with five Republicans opposed, and the House cleared it the following day. The legislation aims to increase housing supply by easing regulations, restrict institutional investors in single-family housing, and give more control to local jurisdictions. President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony, citing a voter ID bill.

items.title: Senate rejects motion on Iran war powers resolution
items.summary: The Senate rejected a motion to proceed on a joint resolution directing removal of U.S. forces from hostilities with Iran.
items.details: On June 24, 2026, the Senate rejected the motion to proceed on S.J.Res. 185 by a vote of 47-50. A similar measure had been approved in the House on June 3 by a vote of 215 to 208. The resolution concerned U.S. military involvement in hostilities against Iran not authorized by Congress.

items.title: Supreme Court allows ending TPS for Haitians and Syrians
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled the administration may end Temporary Protected Status for certain foreign nationals.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, in a 6-3 decision in Mullin v. Doe, the Supreme Court ruled that the president has unreviewable authority to end the Temporary Protected Status program. Justice Samuel Alito wrote for the majority. The decision affects hundreds of thousands of nationals from Haiti and Syria.

items.title: Supreme Court limits Roundup cancer lawsuits
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled Bayer cannot be sued in state courts over failure-to-warn claims about Roundup.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, in a 7-2 ruling, the Supreme Court found that Bayer cannot be sued over state-level claims that it failed to warn of cancer risks from the weedkiller Roundup, holding that federal pesticide law preempts such state labeling claims. The decision is expected to block thousands of lawsuits.

items.title: Supreme Court strikes Hawaii firearm-on-property law
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled states cannot require gun owners to get advance permission to bring firearms onto private property.
items.details: On June 25, 2026, in a 6-3 decision in Wolford v. Lopez, the Supreme Court ruled that requiring permission in advance is an undue burden on the right to possess and carry a firearm. The decision affects laws in states including Hawaii, California, Maryland, New York, and New Jersey.

items.title: Supreme Court allows Texas congressional map for 2026
items.summary: The Supreme Court reinstated Texas's redrawn congressional map for the 2026 midterm elections.
items.details: On April 27, 2026, the Supreme Court granted summary reversal allowing Texas's mid-decade congressional map, signed in August 2025, to remain in effect for the 2026 midterms. The map could shift up to five seats. The court split along ideological lines, with three justices dissenting; related litigation continues.

items.title: Mid-decade redistricting reshapes House map
items.summary: Multiple states have enacted new congressional maps ahead of the 2026 midterms following court rulings and state actions.
items.details: As of June 2026, states including California, Missouri, North Carolina, Ohio, Texas, and Utah had new congressional maps. Following a Supreme Court Voting Rights Act ruling, several Southern states moved to revise districts, while litigation continued in states including Louisiana and New York.

CA.title: Newsom signs election security law before primary
CA.summary: Governor Newsom signed Senate Bill 73 limiting law enforcement access to ballots and voting systems.
CA.details: On May 27, 2026, Governor Gavin Newsom signed SB 73, which takes effect immediately and limits when law enforcement can access ballots, voter lists, rosters, or certified voting technology. The bill followed an incident in which a county sheriff seized ballots from a registrar of voters.

VA.title: General Assembly passes state budget
VA.summary: Virginia's General Assembly passed the Commonwealth's budget, which Governor Spanberger said she supports.
VA.details: Following the General Assembly's passage of the budget on June 22, 2026, Governor Abigail Spanberger issued a statement. The budget process addressed priorities including raises for teachers and public employees, education and infrastructure investment, and data center energy costs.
VA.source: Office of the Governor of Virginia

CT.title: Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Lamont signed legislation establishing youth online safety protections and AI regulations.
CT.details: On June 2, 2026, Governor Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15, a bipartisan measure addressing youth social media protections, artificial intelligence regulation, and workforce training initiatives. Officials cited federal inaction as a reason for state-level action.

IA.title: Reynolds signs final 2026 session bills
IA.summary: Governor Reynolds signed the final bills of the 2026 legislative session, including limits on gubernatorial powers.
IA.details: On June 2, 2026, Governor Kim Reynolds signed the final bills of the session. House File 2694 bars a governor from restricting private businesses or ordering places of worship to close during emergencies and requires legislative approval for changes to election laws. Other bills addressed citizenship verification for educator licenses.

NH.title: Ayotte signs 54 bills into law
NH.summary: Governor Ayotte signed 54 bills into law and issued several vetoes.
NH.details: On June 19, 2026, Governor Kelly Ayotte announced she had signed 54 bills into law covering topics including health benefit plans, water system contamination, and school administrative budgets. She also issued veto statements on several other measures.

OH.title: DeWine signs bills into law
OH.summary: Governor DeWine signed several bills, including the Joshua Alert for missing autistic or developmentally disabled children.
OH.details: On June 18, 2026, Governor Mike DeWine signed multiple bills, including House Bill 359, which clarifies the statewide emergency alert system for missing autistic or developmentally disabled children and names it the Joshua Alert, and measures on construction contracts and unemployment compensation.

IL.title: New Illinois laws take effect July 1
IL.summary: Illinois enacted laws taking effect July 1, including changes to cyberbullying definitions and a motor fuel tax adjustment.
IL.details: Public Act 104-0468, signed in June 2026, suspends the Consumer Price Index adjustment for the motor fuel tax rate until 2027. Other laws taking effect July 1 expand the definition of cyberbullying to address AI content and adjust senior driver's license renewal procedures.

AZ.title: Hobbs vetoes AI bills
AZ.summary: Governor Hobbs vetoed three AI-related bills passed by the legislature.
AZ.details: Around June 19, 2026, Governor Katie Hobbs vetoed three AI bills passed by the Republican-majority legislature, including a chatbot safety measure that had passed the Senate 28-0 and the House 35-20. They were among numerous bills she vetoed.

WA.title: Washington enacts public safety laws
WA.summary: Washington's 2026 session enacted public safety laws, including limits on facial coverings for officers.
WA.details: Washington's 2026 legislative session passed several public safety bills. SB 5855 prohibits law enforcement officers from wearing facial coverings while performing official public-facing duties, with exceptions for medical and protective equipment. Other measures addressed officer training and court procedures.

PA.title: Pennsylvania budget negotiations continue
PA.summary: Lawmakers worked toward a state budget ahead of the June 30 deadline after last year's lengthy impasse.
PA.details: Governor Josh Shapiro presented a $53.3 billion spending plan in February 2026, and negotiations continued as officials eyed the June 30 budget deadline. Lawmakers expressed hope of avoiding a repeat of the prior year's impasse, which stretched more than 130 days past deadline.

RI.title: Rhode Island wraps 2026 legislative session
RI.summary: Rhode Island lawmakers ended their session after passing bills on worker protections and AI regulation.
RI.details: The Rhode Island General Assembly concluded its 2026 session on June 11, 2026, passing bills including warehouse worker protections and an expansion of the Fair Employment Practices Act to cover domestic workers. Governor Dan McKee also signed AI-related legislation.

TN.title: New Tennessee congressional map in place for 2026
TN.summary: Tennessee's legislature passed a new congressional map redrawing the Memphis-area district.
TN.details: Tennessee's Republican-led legislature passed a new congressional map carving up the state's lone majority-Black district in Memphis, pairing urban areas with rural parts of the state. Under the redrawn lines, Tennessee is likely to elect an all-Republican congressional delegation.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and takes no position for or against any party, official, or policy.
